claims.text: Regulators have not concluded MEV is market manipulation; IOSCO treats it as a disclosure/control-risk factor, ESMA's strongest language is hedged, and the SEC's own staff memo distinguishes rule-following searchers/builders from manipulation.
IOSCO's December 2023 DeFi policy report lists MEV strategies as a factor for assessing 'control' over a DeFi arrangement for disclosure purposes, not a manipulation finding.
IOSCO's October 2025 thematic review flags inconsistent MEV handling across custodians/validators as a transparency/conflict-of-interest gap, not a manipulation determination.

ESMA's May 2024 draft MiCA technical-standards consultation uses hedged language ('may be indicative of' market abuse), and industry lawyer Jonathan Galea disputed press characterizations of it as a 'clear example of market abuse'.

The SEC's April 13, 2026 staff statement is about broker-dealer registration of DeFi user-interface/wallet software, not about block builders, validators, or Ethereum ordering mechanics, and is an interim staff position with a built-in five-year sunset.
A May 2025 SEC Crypto Task Force staff memo states that searchers and block builders operating according to understood protocol rules are treated differently from deceptive exploits.
The UBS/Nethermind pilot Mensah cites as proof institutions can already comply was two testnet-only proofs of concept on Sepolia, with zero live transactions.
Titan Builder builds roughly 52-54% of Ethereum blocks as of mid-July 2026, with Quasar next-largest at under 22%.

Lido's Relay Maintenance Committee approved Titan Relay only conditionally, explicitly naming builder+relay vertical integration as a risk and recommending ongoing monitoring rather than a clean bill of health.
A peer-reviewed 2024 paper found the top three builders built roughly 80% of MEV-Boost blocks, driven substantially by exclusive order-flow deals that reduce competition.
Beaverbuild, previously an independent counterweight to Titan, folded its order flow into the multi-party BuilderNet consortium during 2025.
United States v. Peraire-Bueno charges wire fraud and money laundering (not securities/commodities manipulation) over an alleged $25M exploit; the case ended in a mistrial in November 2025.
The Mango Markets (Eisenberg) convictions were fully vacated in May 2025 on improper-venue and failure-to-prove-misrepresentation grounds, with DOJ's appeal still pending.
The Ethereum Foundation's Checkpoint #9 blog (April 2026) describes enshrined-PBS (ePBS) implementation work as 'trickier than anticipated'.

Mensah's co-authored Multi-Party Block Construction proposal, posted May 27, 2026, states plainly that 'today's transaction pipeline has structural gaps that multiple teams and the community have worked to address.'
SUAVE, Flashbots' encrypted-mempool project, was discontinued and its repository archived in May 2025 without ever reaching mainnet.

An October 2025 'TEE.Fail' disclosure demonstrated a hardware-level break capable of undetectable frontrunning against BuilderNet's TDX, a threat Flashbots disputes as impractical to exploit remotely.
The EU's PFOF prohibition under MiFIR Article 39a became absolute on June 30, 2026, when Germany's last transitional exemption expired.
The US SEC proposed rescinding Reg NMS Rules 611 and 610(e), the order-protection framework, on June 11, 2026.
FINRA Rule 5310 imposes a real, litigable best-execution duty on US broker-dealers, and order-routing disclosures do not excuse a firm from that duty.
Sandwich attacks caused an estimated $40-60 million in annual trader losses (Nov 2024-Oct 2025), with monthly extraction falling from roughly $10 million to $2.5 million.
Gattaca was founded in London in 2018, went through Y Combinator, has roughly 19 employees, and one disclosed funding round as of mid-2026.
Searchers reportedly pay more than 90% of gross arbitrage revenue upstream to block proposers and builders.
Ethereum's own core developers and Mensah's own separate technical writing both describe today's builder/relay system as having 'structural gaps'.
Loss-versus-rebalancing (LVR) research estimates upwards of $600 million extracted from Ethereum liquidity providers, an order of magnitude larger than sandwich losses.

Budish, Cramton & Shim (QJE 2015) analyze a continuous-time, cross-venue latency-arbitrage problem cleared by a neutral, unforkable exchange, and propose discrete-time batch auctions replacing speed competition with price competition within that trusted-clearinghouse setting.
Research on on-chain batch auctions finds that 'the block builder's current ability to rearrange the batch content is certainly sufficient to extract value.'
Paradigm's foundational MEV research argues a bot that arbitrages a market back to parity 'makes the market more efficient without harming the original trader.'
